rationale: X makes a factual error claiming no CPU-level test exists for endpoint restrictions and asking to add one to tests/config/test_endpoint_policy.py, when that exact file with exactly those CPU tests is created in this diff — a serious grounding failure. X also flags the trust_remote_code default flip as an unintentional regression to revert, when the ground truth shows the author deliberately changed it for safety reasons (never default trust_remote_code to True). Y correctly identifies the trust_remote_code default change as a real but likely-intentional/positive change worth confirming, accurately describes the deploy-override endpoint-restriction fix (the top concern in the human review thread) and its regression test, and correctly notes the CPU unit test coverage. Both reviews miss several GT concerns (hf_config cache mutability, positional-arg signature risk, create_error_response usage, CI/async engine errors, auditing other endpoints), but Y's findings are better grounded and less contradicted by the visible diff.
